coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from spent coffee grounds via ball-milling: Application in fluorescent chemosensors
coredata.dc:creator: Jeong, Gwajeong
Park, Chan Ho
Yi, Dongchan
Yang, Hyunseung
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are nanoparticles that have gained widespread attention due to their unique properties including multicolored fluorescence, photochemical stability, and biocompatibility. However, the conventional approaches for the preparation of CDs typically require high energy input and toxic reagents. Herein, a sustainable, high throughput approach is reported for producing uniform CDs. Specifically, spent coffee grounds (SCGs) are used to produce coffee ground-derived carbon dots (CFCDs) via a simple wet ball-milling process. The versatility of our approach can be extended to the synthesis of heteroatom-doped CDs and the preparation of CDs from various SCG sources. Also, the functional groups of the CFCDs are manipulated by the addition of citric acids during the ball-milling process to produce carboxylic acid-functionalized carbon dots (CA-CFCDs). CA-CFCDs exhibit highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution via fluorescence quenching, and the relevant quenching mechanism is investigated. Finally, we demonstrate the fluorescent microgel sensor exhibiting a rapid, ratiometric fluorescence change upon the addition of Fe3+ ion by encapsulating the CA-CFCDs within the microgel matrix.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from spent coffee grounds via ball-milling: Application in fluorescent chemosensors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are nanoparticles that have gained widespread attention due to their unique properties including multicolored fluorescence, photochemical stability, and biocompatibility. However, the conventional approaches for the preparation of CDs typically require high energy input and toxic reagents. Herein, a sustainable, high throughput approach is reported for producing uniform CDs. Specifically, spent coffee grounds (SCGs) are used to produce coffee ground-derived carbon dots (CFCDs) via a simple wet ball-milling process. The versatility of our approach can be extended to the synthesis of heteroatom-doped CDs and the preparation of CDs from various SCG sources. Also, the functional groups of the CFCDs are manipulated by the addition of citric acids during the ball-milling process to produce carboxylic acid-functionalized carbon dots (CA-CFCDs). CA-CFCDs exhibit highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution via fluorescence quenching, and the relevant quenching mechanism is investigated. Finally, we demonstrate the fluorescent microgel sensor exhibiting a rapid, ratiometric fluorescence change upon the addition of Fe3+ ion by encapsulating the CA-CFCDs within the microgel matrix.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention as a new class of novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterials due to their distinct features relative to those of conventional probe molecules, including tunable fluorescence, exceptional photostability, and low toxicity (Li et al., 2015; Semeniuk et al., 2019). These fascinating features render the CDs suitable for various applications in bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, and sensing devices (Gong et al., 2022; Li et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019, 2017; B. Wu et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2023). Owing to their high potential in the field of applied technology, various methods have been proposed for the synthesis of CDs, including laser ablation (Hu et al., 2009; Sun et al., 2006), electrochemical methods (Zhao et al., 2008; Zheng et al., 2009), hydrothermal methods (Yang et al., 2011, 2012), microwave-assisted synthesis (Qu et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2011; Zhu et al., 2009), and chemical oxidation (Hu et al., 2014). The majority of those methods have been developed, focusing on synthesizing CDs with high quantum yield or synthesizing CDs in a more facile and rapid manner. However, they tentatively incur various environmental issues since conventional methodologies require the use of petroleum-based precursors, large amounts of organic solvents, and strong acids. Further, they suffer from limitations of complicated and expensive instrument usage and energy-extensive consumption during the synthetic process of CDs. Recently, several groups have attempted to develop eco-friendly methods for the synthesis of CDs using bioresources as precursors (Huang et al., 2018; Jeong et al., 2018; Romero et al., 2019; Suryawanshi et al., 2014; Wareing et al., 2021). Despite rapid progress achieved in the synthesis of CDs, but, still, it is challenging to establish a green process to produce highly efficient CDs satisfying key aspects including i) the use of nontoxic reagents, ii) an energy-efficient synthetic process minimizing carbon emission (e.g., low thermal/pressure conditions), and iii) quality control from the biomass. Given these, discovering ideal precursors and developing green methods for the synthesis of CDs has been considered as a long-sought goal.

Spent coffee grounds (SCGs), i.e., the residue obtained during the coffee brewing process, are one of the great candidates as a recyclable bioresource. Although SCGs are the most abundant coffee by-product that over 6 million tons have been annually generated worldwide since the 2010s due to increasing global coffee demand (Janissen and Huynh, 2018; Mussatto et al., 2011), a great majority of SCGs are simply discarded potentially having an adverse impact on the environment (Blinová et al., 2017; Mussatto et al., 2011). In this regard, there have been interesting attempts to convert SCGs into value-added products including fluorescent nanoprobes. For example, Chang et al. were the first to synthesize CDs from SCGs via heating and a simple separation process (Hsu et al., 2012), and to demonstrate that the SCGs-derived CDs can be employed as efficient fluorescent probes for the imaging of cells and matrices. Hakkarainen's group and Yang's group successfully synthesized coffee ground-derived CDs using a hydrothermal process (Hong and Yang, 2021; Xu et al., 2017). It was also demonstrated that the carbonization of SCGs can produce fluorescent CDs displaying their potential application as sensing probes for metal ions in water (Crista et al., 2020). Although these reports successfully demonstrated the synthesis of CDs by utilizing SCGs as green precursors, it still remained a major challenge to achieve facile, nonhazardous, easily scalable, and quality controllable systems for the production of CDs to fulfill both industrial and environmental requirements.

In this regard, we focus on the ball-milling approach which is one of the best approaches in terms of a simple and environmentally benign process. Ball-milling applies mechanical energy to break the chemical bonds and fracture the particles without the use of hazardous wet chemicals and has therefore attracted significant attention for the exfoliation of graphitic layers and surface functionalization of graphene nanoplatelets. For example, CDs/graphene quantum dots were successfully synthesized from activated carbon powders or graphites by using ball-milling in previous reports (Ge et al., 2019; Han et al., 2018; Martín-Pacheco et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2015, 2016; Youh et al., 2020, 2021). Nevertheless, so far, non-renewable and non-sustainable carbon sources were mainly highlighted as precursors for synthesizing CDs based on the ball-milling process. Moreover, the production yield and optical properties of CDs in terms of ball-milling conditions (e.g., ball-milling time) were not deeply discussed, which further restricts the practical applications of CDs.

Herein, we develop an energy-efficient and reproducible way to synthesize CDs from recycling SCGs as a carbon source under mild conditions without the use of any harsh reagents. As shown in Fig. 1 a, a wet ball-milling technique is utilized to produce coffee ground-derived carbon dots (CFCDs) and to control the surface property including functional groups and hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity. We also explore the system to generate the carboxylic acid-functionalized carbon dots (CA-CFCDs) and nitrogen-containing CFCDs (N-CFCDs) through the addition of oxygen/nitrogen-containing small molecules during the ball-milling process. As-prepared CDs are extraordinarily small with diameters of less than 2 nm, and whole synthetic processes and product quality of CDs are readily reproducible. Compared to the un-modified CFCDs, the CA-CFCDs and N-CFCDs display exceptional dispersion stability in hydrophilic solvents. Furthermore, the metal-ion sensing activity of the CA-CFCDs is systematically investigated. Among various metal ions, the CA-CFCDs exhibit highly selective and sensitive detection of ferric ions (Fe3+) in an aqueous solution. Also, CA-CFCDs exhibit their Fe3+ selective sensing behavior under various counter anions. To take advantage of this strong fluorescence and sensing activity, a fluorescent chemosensor platform with rapid response behavior is fabricated by embedding the as-prepared CA-CFCDs in a microgel matrix to provide a consistent fluorescence intensity regardless of the surrounding conditions.

Roasted whole bean coffee (Breakfast blend, medium roast) was purchased from Starbucks Coffee Company. Roasted coffee beans were ground by De'longhi Dinamica automatic coffee machine. Citric acid was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethanol was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Tetrahydrofuran (THF) was purchased from Honeywell Burdick & Jackson. Fe(NO3)3, Zn(NO3)2, CuCl2, MnCl2, and MgCl2 were purchased from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., LTD. NiCl2 and AlCl3 were purchased from Samchun Chemicals Co., LTD. NdCl3, FeCl3, Fe2(SO4)3, Al(NO3)3, AlCl3 and Al2(SO4)3 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate (Mn = 700), sodium acrylate, and lithium phenyl-2,4,6-trimethylbenzoylphosphinate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Double distilled water (DI water) was used in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CFCDs, CA-CFCDs and N-CFCDs
ce:section.ce:para: First, the SCGs were dried at 80 °C for 48 h to remove any moisture. Then, the SCGs (10 g), ethanol (50 mL), and 5-mm diameter, wear-resistant zirconia balls (40 g) were poured into a 250-mL PET plastic container, and the ball-milling process was performed at 150 rpm at room temperature. The resulting black solution was then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to remove any aggregated particles, followed by dialysis in DI water for 2 days using tubing with a molecular-weight cutoff (MWCO) of 500 Da. The pure CFCDs were then obtained after drying. Similar procedures were used to prepare the CA-CFCDs with the addition of citric acid (1 g) during the ball-milling process. To investigate the effect of ball-milling time on the emission behavior and production yield of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs, all other conditions (e.g., ball size and reaction medium) were maintained constant while milling was conducted for 6, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h. The synthesis process of N-CFCDs was similar to that of CA-CFCDs, except for the addition of o-phenylenediamine instead of citric acid.

The CA-CFCDs were added to solutions of various metal ions in DI water (1.0 mg/mL), including CuCl2, MnCl2, MgCl2, NdCl3, NiCl2, AlCl3, Zn(NO3)2, and Fe(NO3)3. The metal ion concentration was maintained at 10 mM. In addition, aqueous solutions containing 0.1–5 mM Fe3+ were added to the CA-CFCDs (1.0 mg/mL) in order to investigate the effect of Fe3+ ion concentration upon the fluorescence behavior. The fluorescence spectrum of each solution was recorded at 25 °C in the emission range of 380–700 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. To investigate the effect of anions on the fluorescence behavior of CA-CFCDs, aqueous solutions of cations and anions including Fe(NO3)3, FeCl3, Fe2(SO4)3, Al(NO3)3, AlCl3 and Al2(SO4)3 were prepared by adding various concentrations of Fe3+ ion (0.1–6.0 mM) to CA-CFCDs solution (0.1 mg/mL). Then, the fluorescence spectra were gathered at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of microgel particles containing CA-CFCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the use of the CDs-based probes in environmental and biological applications, microgels containing the CA-CFCDs were prepared by the following route. Mixtures of poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate (8.035 μL) and sodium acrylate (1 mg) were dissolved in a 10-μL CA-CFCDs solution of (10 mg/mL) in PBS with a pH of 7.4 to form a pre-hydrogel solution. Then, lithium phenyl-2,4,6-trimethylbenzoylphosphinate (0.025 wt%) was added as the photoinitiator, and the mixture was degassed. The resulting pre-hydrogel solution was then dropped onto a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) mold with a cylindrical hole array which was fabricated using previously-reported procedures (Park et al., 2020). The pre-hydrogel solution was infiltrated into the cylindrical holes with the aid of physical pressure and vacuum for 1 min, after which the residual pre-hydrogel solution was thoroughly removed by blading. After photopolymerization of the pre-hydrogel solution under UV irradiation for 30 s, the photocured microgels were detached from the PDMS mold. The microgel particles containing the CA-CFCDs were then obtained after rinsing with DI water.

The as-prepared CFCD and CA-CFCD solutions were purified via filtration and dialysis to eliminate any impurities, including unreacted citric acid. The pure CFCDs and CA-CFCDs were then analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-F200, JEOL). The sizes and carbon lattices of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs were analyzed in order to confirm their successful production. The uniform particle sizes were further examined via dynamic light scattering (DLS; ZEN3600, Malvern) measurements of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs dispersed in ethanol. The average height of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs was measured via atomic force microscopy (AFM; XE-150, Park Systems). It is noteworthy that the diameter of CDs measured with AFM can be overestimated due to the AFM tip convolution effect (Dsouza et al., 2021). Thus, we considered only the height of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs using AFM in this study. The surface chemical compositions of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs were characterized by Fourier-transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Scientific). The elemental compositions and presence of functional groups in the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs were examined via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Nexsa, Thermo Scientific), and the effect of citric acid upon the crystallinity of the CFCDs was examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (SmartLab, Rigaku) and Raman spectroscopy (Confotec MR520, Sol Instruments). Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR; JNM-ESC400, JEOL) analysis was conducted to estimate the chemical structures of CA-CFCDs. The optical properties of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs were examined by ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy (Cary 60 UV–Vis spectrophotometer, Agilent Technologies) and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy (PTI QuantaMaster 400, Horiba). The corresponding PL quantum yields were determined with respect to the fluorescence standard, Rhodamine B (Song et al., 2017). In addition, the photostability of CFCDs and CA-CFCDs was evaluated by irradiating the CDs solution under 365 nm with the intensity of 300 μW/cm2 for 120 min. Life cycle assessment (LCA) of synthesizing CFCDs and CA-CFCDs was carried out following ISO14048 standards and using Total (version 6.6.2) software. The global warming potential (GWP) was particularly evaluated using impact assessment methodologies (EDP_2013 GWP). The life cycle inventory data consisted of the Ecoinvent database and the LCI DB by the Korea Ministry of Environment and Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy were utilized (Arvidsson et al., 2014; Baragau et al., 2021; Beloin-Saint-Pierre and Hischier, 2021; Fernandes et al., 2021; Munuera et al., 2021; Sendão et al., 2020).

Inspired by the fact that SCGs undergo a heating process during coffee roasting which might induce conditions similar to the pyrolytic decomposition process in typical CDs synthesis, we suggest the synthetic mechanism to produce the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs via the wet ball-milling method from SCGs in Fig. S1. Polysaccharides, the most abundant components in coffee seed and silverskin (Ballesteros et al., 2014), are aromatized and condensed during the coffee heat roasting and brewing processes, forming local graphitic structures (Cheng and Li, 2018). Therefore, pre-roasted coffee beans contain the intermediate structures to be extracted as CDs components. Then, the following ball-milling process generates physical stress and local heat on SCG particles by the collision of zirconia balls. This process simultaneously condenses and breaks the SCG surface involving the intermediates and extracts the CFCDs with pure ball-milling media and CA-CFCDs with citric acid additives.

The TEM images in Fig. 1b and e indicate that the obtained CFCDs and CA-CFCDs have uniform sizes and spherical shapes, with average diameters of 1.59 ± 0.07 nm and 1.64 ± 0.04 nm, respectively. This uniformity in size and shape is further confirmed by the DLS results in Fig. S2 of the supporting information. In detail, the as-prepared CFCDs and CA-CFCDs have hydrodynamic diameters of 4.64 ± 2.11 nm and 2.67 ± 1.65 nm, respectively, thus indicating that they are highly monodisperse without any aggregation. Further, the HR-TEM images in Fig. 1c and f reveal that both the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs have a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, suggesting the presence of ordered graphene (100) facets (Zhang et al., 2016). In addition, the AFM images in Fig. 1d and g indicate that the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs have average heights of 2.55 ± 0.01 nm and 3.10 ± 0.01 nm, respectively. This is attributed to the chain length of the citric acid grafted on the CA-CFCDs.

The FT-IR spectra of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs are presented in Fig. 2 a. Here, the CFCDs exhibit peaks at 2849 and 2921 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching vibrations of the C–H group (Shuang et al., 2018). In addition, the absorption peak at 1701 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the CO group, and the weak band at 1653 cm−1 reflects the CC stretching vibration of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (Hu et al., 2015). However, upon the addition of citric acid during the ball-milling process, the broad peaks at 3353 and 3190 cm−1 corresponding to the characteristic O–H and N–H stretching vibrations are significantly increased in the CA-CFCDs, thus suggesting the presence of sufficient surface carboxylic acid groups (Konar et al., 2019). Further, the XPS survey spectra in Fig. S3 indicate that both the CFCDs and the CA-CFCDs exhibit characteristic peaks of C1s, N1s, and O1s at about 287, 402, and 535 eV, respectively. As indicated in Table S1, the atomic ratios of C/O according to XPS were calculated to be 8.68 and 1.43 for the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs, respectively, thus indicating that the CA-CFCDs have more hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups. In addition, while the C1s XPS spectrum of the CFCDs in Fig. 2b exhibits peaks at 284.5, 286.4, and 289.1 eV due to the C–C, C–O–C, and O–CO group, respectively (Compton et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2015), the peaks at 286.4 and 289.1 eV are significantly increased in intensity for the CA-CFCDs (Fig. 2c). This result confirms the successful grafting of the citric acid molecules during the ball-milling process. 1H-NMR analysis was also conducted to estimate the presence of several important chemical structures within CA-CFCDs using deuterium oxide (D2O) as a solvent. Fig. S4 represents that CA-CFCDs have broad peaks between 1 and 3 ppm possibly due to the presence of sp3 C–H protons. Additionally, peaks that appeared between 3 and 6 ppm may be originated from protons attached to oxygen-containing groups including carboxylic acids. Furthermore, signals from aromatic hydrogen are detected in the range of 6–8 ppm (De and Karak, 2013; Yang et al., 2019).

The XRD patterns of the CFCDs and the CA-CFCDs are presented in Fig. 2d. Here, the CFCDs exhibit a broad diffraction peak at 18.8°, originated from partially disordered graphitic structures (Hou et al., 2016). This peak is shifted to a slightly lower angle of 17.6° for the CA-CFCDs due to conjugation and intercalation of the citric acid between the CFCD layers, thus decreasing the order of crystallinity of the graphite (Daud et al., 2017). This effect is further demonstrated by the Raman spectra of the CFCD and CA-CFCDs in Fig. 2e and f. Here, both spectra exhibit two exceptional peaks at around 1380 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 due to the D and G bands, respectively. These results suggest the coexistence of sp2 graphitic carbon and sp3 carbon defects within the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs. In particular, the intensity ratio of the D and G band signals (ID/IG) is seen to be slightly increased from 0.81 to 0.86 with the addition of citric acid during the ball-milling process. This is attributed to the destruction of the graphitic crystalline network of the CFCDs due to the grafting of citric acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties are among the most fascinating features of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, making them useful for optics-related applications. Hence, the optical properties of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs are revealed by the UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 3 a. Here, both spectra exhibit strong absorption at 230–330 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains and the n-π* transition of the CO bonds (Yang et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2018). Interestingly, an increase in the intensity of the peak associated with the n–π* transition of CO bonds at 329 nm is observed for the CA-CFCDs, which is probably related to an increase in the degree of oxidation due to the grafting of citric acid during the ball-milling process (Bai et al., 2019). Further, the PL emission behavior of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs at 360 nm excitation is revealed in Fig. 3b, where CFCDs and CA-CFCDs exhibit strong PL emission peaks centered at 462 nm and 454 nm, respectively. The PL quantum yields of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs were determined to be 3.71% and 3.95%, respectively, which is comparable to those of other CDs derived from food sources (Cong et al., 2018; Liao et al., 2015). In addition, the exceptional photostability of CFCDs and CA-CFCDs relative to that of the conventional organic dye fluorescein is demonstrated in Fig. S5, where the initial PL emission of the fluorescein is seen to decrease by about 50% over the course of time, while no significant photobleaching is observed for the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs. Further, Fig. 3c and d indicate that CFCDs and CA-CFCDs showed similar PL emission peak positions, regardless of the ball-milling times. We also estimated the production yield based on the amounts of CFCDs and CA-CFCDs obtained from 10 g of SCGs (four batches each). As the reaction time increases, little increase in the production yield of CFCDs was observed, with a maximum yield of about 1.00% (100 mg CFCDs). By contrast, the yield of CA-CFCDs was seen to increase as the reaction time increased from 6 to 72 h, achieving a maximum production yield of 4.51% (496 mg CA-CFCDs). However, little further increase in the yield of CA-CFCDs was observed when the ball-milling was conducted for longer than 72 h. Further, when the milling process was conducted for 72 h in the absence of any balls, the yield of CA-CFCDs was estimated to be 46 mg. This result demonstrates that the ball-milling process is crucial to the preparation of CA-CFCDs from SCGs. Therefore, the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs obtained via 72 h ball-milling were selected for further experiments.

As with previously reported luminescent carbon nanomaterials, the results in Figs. S6a and b demonstrate excitation wavelength-dependent PL emission behavior for the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs, with a shift of the emission peaks towards longer wavelengths as the excitation is increased from 340 to 500 nm. This is well-known to be due to the multiple energy bandgaps originating from diverse surface defect sites of CDs (Lecroy et al., 2017; Schneider et al., 2017). The observed difference between the excitation dependency of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs probably depends on whether defect sites are present or passivated by citric acids. Thus, the position of peak PL emission of the CFCDs drastically increases from 440 nm to 538 nm as the excitation wavelength is increased from 340 to 500 nm, whereas that of CA-CFCDs gradually increases from 448 nm to 520 nm as the excitation wavelength is increased from 340 to 500 nm (Fig. 3e and f). This suggests that the majority of energy bandgaps in the CA-CFCDs are larger than those in the CFCDs. Hence, it is speculated that oxygen-containing citric acid-derived groups in the CA-CFCDs introduce numerous small subdomains with sp2 graphitic carbon structures in place of large carbon domains, thereby increasing the local band gaps. This hypothesis also explains the observed changes in the PL emission intensities of the CDs over various wavelengths (Yoon et al., 2016, 2020). Specifically, while the PL intensity of the CFCDs is maintained as the excitation wavelength is increased from 340 to 460 nm, a rapid decline in PL intensity is observed for the CA-CFCDs at an excitation at 380 nm because the majority of small subdomains in the CA-CFCDs are energetically favorable to be excited under the relatively shorter excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Versatility and applicability of the proposed approach
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the versatility of our approach for producing heteroatom-doped CDs, we prepared nitrogen-doped CFCDs (N-CFCDs) through the ball-milling process in the presence of SCGs and o-phenylenediamine, where o-phenylenediamine serves as nitrogen resource. The TEM image shows that the average size of the N-CFCDs is 4.01 ± 0.31 nm. HR-TEM images reveal the high crystallinity of N-CFCDs (Fig. S7a). Further, the obtained N-CFCDs showed exceptional dispersibility in ethanol. Particularly, N-CFCDs showed greenish emissions under UV light excitation, with a higher PL quantum yield of 7.36% compared to that of CA-CFCDs (Fig. S7b). We suggest that the higher PL quantum yield of the N-CFCDs originated from nitrogen doping during the ball-milling process. Particularly, N-CFCDs show a strong emission at 510 nm with an excitation-independent PL behavior, which is one of the common features of nitrogen-doped CDs (Baragau et al., 2020; Moniruzzaman and Kim, 2019). (Fig. S7c) We also conducted the ball-milling process using SCGs obtained from the different local coffee brand, and the results are presented in Fig. S8. As-prepared CDs exhibit similar UV–Vis absorption and PL emission with a PL quantum yield of 7.82%. The higher PL quantum yield of the CDs synthesized using SCGs of a local coffee brand, relative to that of the above-mentioned CFCDs and CA-CFCDs, may be ascribed to differences in the coffee-bean roasting conditions, which determine the degree of pyrolytic decomposition. These above results demonstrate the universality of our approach to the synthesis of heteroatom-doped CDs and the preparation of CDs from various SCG sources.

In addition, the solubilities of the CFCDs and CA-CFCDs in various solvents, including water, ethanol, DMF, THF, chloroform, and toluene are compared in Fig. 4 . The CFCDs are seen to be readily dispersed in hydrophobic solvents but are insoluble in water due to their relatively strong hydrophobic character. By contrast, the CA-CFCDs are hydrophilic due to the presence of carboxylic acid groups on their surface; hence, they are readily dispersed in water without any aggregation, but are not dispersible in hydrophobic solvents. Diverse CDs with tailored surface properties are highly demanded for various applications, including hydrophobic CDs for organic optoelectronics (Benetti et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2017), and hydrophilic CDs for bioimaging probes (Bhatt et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2021; Yue et al., 2020) and environmental sensors (Cayuela et al., 2015; Fan et al., 2017). Although several surface modification approaches, including encapsulation of CDs with amphiphilic polymers and chemical grafting have been suggested in order to meet this requirement (Fowley et al., 2012; Kim et al., 2017), these approaches are complicated by their requirements for additional post-synthetic surface modifications. In this regard, the proposed ball-milling of SCGs with the addition of citric acid is a straightforward, one-step approach to the preparation of CDs with controlled surface properties.

Streamlined LCA was also carried out to quantify the green engineering and sustainability of our CDs synthetic approach. As summarized in Figs. S9a and b, our CDs synthetic process consists of five electricity-based unit processes (drying(1) - ball milling - drying(2) - centrifugation - dialysis). Fig. S9c shows that the portion of electricity in GWP value for producing CFCDs and CA-CFCDs was 57% and 48%, respectively, indicating that the process-driven GWP was as large as the raw material-driven GWP portion in both cases. Particularly, the Electricity(2) which corresponds to the required energy amounts in the ball milling process merely affected global warming (only ∼1%), suggesting that the CDs extraction step was efficiently processed without massive energy usage in this work. We rationally expect further potential to reduce the GWP by improving the drying process and solvent usage, which are the majority of GWP in our systems. Also, we analyzed our green synthetic system by comparing our CDs to other CDs and graphene materials in terms of consumed electricity and GWP (Table S2). In contrast to the other CDs and graphene synthetic approaches, our approach showed moderate consumption of electricity and carbon emission due to the low processing temperature and simple process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of metal ions: selectivity and sensitivity
ce:section.ce:para: The use of CDs for the selective fluorescent sensing of specific metal ions has attracted much attention in the fields of environmental protection and bioanalysis. This sensing effect is mainly related to the interaction of metal ions with the fluorescent surface states of the CDs, followed by static or dynamic fluorescence quenching, photoinduced electron transfer, the inner filter effect, or energy transfer processes (Zu et al., 2017). Due to their exceptional water solubility, the CA-CFCDs were selected for evaluation as metal-ion sensing fluorescent probes by monitoring their fluorescence changes in the presence of various common metal ions (Cu2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Nd3+, Ni2+, Al3+, Zn2+, and Fe3+). The results in Fig. 5 a indicate that the PL intensity of the CA-CFCDs is effectively quenched by Fe3+ ions, whereas the same concentration of other metal ions leads to low or negligible quenching. This selectivity towards Fe3+ ions is most likely due to the strong binding and rapid chelation kinetics of the Fe3+ ion with the oxygen-containing functional groups on the CA-CFCDs (X. Wu et al., 2019; Zulfajri et al., 2019). The process is demonstrated visually in Fig. 5b, which shows photographic images of the fluorescence from an aqueous CA-CFCD solution before and after the addition of various 10 mM metal-ion solutions under 365 nm UV irradiation. Here, the bright blue color of the CA-CFCDs solution is seen to fade distinctly with the addition of Fe3+, whereas the original color is maintained in the presence of other metal ions. These results indicate that the CA-CFCDs are suitable for application as specific, efficient, and selective optical sensors for Fe3+ due to the stronger affinity of this ion.

The sensitivity of the CA-CFCDs for detecting Fe3+ ions in an aqueous solution is demonstrated by the fluorescence changes in the presence of various concentrations of Fe3+ ions in the range of 0–5.0 mM. The photographic images in Fig. 6 a indicate that the greenish-blue fluorescence of the aqueous CA-CFCD solution gradually quenches with increasing Fe3+ concentration, which is reflected by a gradual decrease in intensity of the PL spectra in Fig. 6b. The interaction between the CA-CFCDs and Fe3+ is determined by plotting and analyzing the intensity ratio (I
0 /I) curve according to the Stern-Volmer equation, i.e. (Geddes, 2001):
I
0 /I = (1 + K
D [Q]) (1 + K
S [Q])
Where I
0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of the CA-CFCDs in the absence and presence of the Fe3+, respectively, K
D and K
S are the dynamic and static quenching constant, and [Q] is the Fe3+ concentration (Lakowicz, 2006). As shown in Fig. 6c, the I
0 /I curve exhibits a linear relationship at lower Fe3+ concentrations (0–2 mM) that can be well fitted with K
D = 1880 M−1 and K
S = 0 M−1. The limit of detection (LOD) can be calculated using the equation LOD = 3σ/k, where σ and s stand for the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 6) and slope of the linear fitting curve, respectively. The calculated LOD was 2.25 μM. At higher Fe3+ concentrations, an upward curvature in the Stern-Volmer curve suggests that static and dynamic quenching processes occur simultaneously in the CA-CFCDs, with a K
D and K
S of 1880 and 302 M−1, respectively. Taken together, these results indicate that dynamic quenching is the dominant PL mechanism at low Fe3+ concentrations, when the metal ions are weakly coupled with the CA-CFCDs, while a combination of dynamic and static quenching occurs at high Fe3+ concentrations due to physical association and complexation of the ions with CA-CFCDs (Park et al., 2015). (Fig. 7 a and b) According to the guidelines for drinking water quality reported by World Health Organization (WHO), the standard concentration of iron ion (Fe2+/Fe3+) in water is 5.4 μM (0.3 mg/L). Above this concentration, it can be determined as unqualified water for drinking and domestic water (Ji et al., 2019; WHO, 2017). In this sense, the response range and LOD of our sensing probe are qualified for determining the water quality. Overall, in terms of synthetic conditions (the temperature in production = room temperature, and the solvent = ethanol), mass production (>400 mg), and sensing performance (LOD = 2.25 μM), our approach is promising for the production of CDs for Fe3+ sensing probe compared to previously reported CDs-based Fe3+ sensing. (Table S3 and Fig. 7c)

Moreover, we investigate the anion dependency of CA-CFCDs on their optical response toward to Fe3+ ions. The NO3−, Cl−, and SO4 − are selected as model counter anions of Fe3+ in sample solutions, and sample solutions containing Al3+ with various counter anions are compared simultaneously. The optical responses are analyzed by monitoring the PL intensity ratios (I
0 /I at 360 nm) and the absorption changes. The absorbance difference of CA-CFCD in the presence of ions was plotted at 329 nm (a local peak wavelength) by subtracting the absorbance value of the ion-dissolved aqueous solution to ion-dissolved CA-CFCDs solution to eliminate the absorption from ionized water (Figs. S10 and 11). As summarized in Fig. 8 , when the Al3+ is added to the CA-CFCD solution, the I
0 /I is around 1 and absorption spectra are stationary. These observations indicate that Al3+ negligibly affects to the optical property of CA-CFCD regardless of the counter anions. In contrast, the I
0 /I and the absorption are dramatically changed in the presence of the Fe3+ for three different counter anions compared to the Al3+. In particular, above the concentration of 2 mM Fe3+, it is observed that the degree of quenching is highly depends on the type of counter anions. This observation suggests that the dynamic quenching with Fe3+ in low concentrations is not significantly affected by the counter anions while counter anions may affect physical association and complexation between Fe3+ ions and CA-CFCDs at high Fe3+ concentrations, thereby affecting the static quenching process. Moreover, the changes in I
0 /I and the absorption of CA-CFCDs solutions with Fe2(SO4)3 are relatively lower than that of CA-CFCDs solutions with Fe(NO3) and FeCl3. We speculate that this observation is probably correlated to the polarizability of anions representing the reactivity of ion pairs in solution (the polarizability of the anion: SO4 −: 5.47 × 10−30 m3, NO3 −: 4.13 × 10−30 m3, and Cl−: 3.42 × 10−30 m3) (Wang et al., 2021). From these above observations, it can be concluded that CA-CFCDs obviously maintain their Fe3+ selective quenching behavior under various counter anions, which is a great advantage for their usage in an ambient and practical environment that may possess various types of anions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CA-CFCDs-based microgels for environmental and biological applications
ce:section.ce:para: The use of fluorescent nanoprobes in the environment is challenged by the following issues: (i) the dilution of the nanoprobes in fluids reduces the signal-to-noise ratio, thus leading to inaccurate measurements, and (ii) the biofouling and undesired interaction between ambient molecules degrade the photonic and sensing properties of the probes. To address these issues and, thus, preserve the intrinsic optical properties, the CA-CFCDs were encapsulated in microgel particles in accordance with previous work (Park et al., 2018, 2020), and as shown schematically in Fig. 9 a. As observed by the fluorescence optical microscopy images in Fig. 9b and c, cylindrical-shaped microgels were fabricated with a diameter of 50 μm and a height of 50 μm, with each microgel particle exhibiting strong fluorescence due to the CA-CFCDs. Furthermore, microgel particles exhibit a uniform fluorescence intensity without significant microgel-to-microgel variation. Notably, no measurable fluorescence was observed in the water-swollen pristine microgels without any CA-CFCDs (Fig. S12). These results indicate that the CA-CFCDs were homogeneously dispersed within the microgels; thus, the probe concentration and its average fluorescence were maintained in the fluid over the experiments. This exceptional dispersion is attributed to hydrogen bonding interactions between the microgel matrix and the hydroxyl/carboxyl groups on the surface of CA-CFCDs. As with the above-mentioned results for the CA-CFCDs in aqueous Fe3+ solution, Fig. 9d and e reveal the fading of the fluorescent blue emission within 16s of adding Fe3+ ions to the microgels suspended in water. This demonstrates that the microgel-encapsulated CA-CFCDs are a promising material system for developing reliable fluorescent probes with rapid response times for detecting Fe3+ ions under various environmental conditions in practical applications.

Herein, a simple, environmentally-friendly, and scalable approach was developed to produce fluorescent CDs from SCGs. The CDs were synthesized at room temperature with ethanol using the ball-milling method. The surface property of CDs was controlled by the addition of citric acid during the ball-milling process. The as-prepared CFCDs and CA-CFCDs are observed to have average sizes of 1.59 ± 0.07 nm and 1.64 ± 0.04 nm, respectively, and exhibit uniform emission wavelength (462 and 454 nm, respectively), with a quantum yield of 3–4%. Although the PL quantum yield of CFCDs and CA-CFCDs in our study is slightly lower when compared to other CDs prepared by conventional synthetic methods, this can be resolved by the addition of appropriate dopants which can tailor the optical properties of CDs during the ball-milling process. The selective binding of the Fe3+ ion was shown to induce the fluorescence quenching of the CA-CFCDs, thereby providing highly sensitive probes (LOD = 2.25 μM) for the detection of this ion in an aqueous solution. Fluorescent studies suggested that the fluorescence changes in the CA-CFCDs upon adding Fe 3+ ion originate from the dynamic quenching mechanism at low Fe3+ concentrations and a combination of dynamic and static quenching mechanisms at high Fe3+ concentrations. The CA-CFCDs maintained their Fe3+ selective quenching behavior under various counter anions including NO3−, Cl−, and SO4 2−, and the degree of reactivity followed the trend of polarizability of anions. This remarkable sensing behavior was successfully combined with a polymeric matrix by the preparation of microgels containing the CA-CFCDs. As with the CA-CFCDs in an aqueous solution, the obtained microgels were able to detect Fe3+ ions via fluorescence changes within a few seconds, thus demonstrating their potential practical application as a credible fluorescent probe for the detection of metal ions. Hence, this work opens up a new sustainable approach to the preparation of CDs and provides a unique insight into the utilization of SCGs.

Gwajeong Jeong: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Chan Ho Park: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation. Dongchan Yi: Investigation, Resources. Hyunseung Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
